title: The Accidental Infrastructure: How Crypto Miners Built the Foundation of the AI Boom - Semiwiki
gist: Former Ethereum/Bitcoin miners (CoreWeave, Crusoe, Core Scientific, Marathon, Riot) accidentally built the GPU-dense, power-contracted infrastructure the AI boom needed, giving them an ~18-month head start and turning 'compute real estate' into a $200B+ asset class deeply financed by, and strategically allied with, Nvidia.

themes.theme: GPU Hardware Lineage: Gaming to Crypto to AI

insights.claim: Ethereum's Ethash proof-of-work was deliberately engineered to be ASIC-resistant by requiring large amounts of fast memory alongside computation, which kept general-purpose Nvidia and AMD GPUs competitive in mining for nearly a decade rather than ceding the network to custom-chip vendors.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: The AI infrastructure edge held by ex-miners traces to a design choice, not luck; understanding it helps assess how durable GPU-cluster operating expertise really is.
insights.quote: Its proof-of-work algorithm, Ethash, was engineered to be ASIC-resistant.
insights.locator: Bitcoin mining split early into two hardware paths

insights.claim: Bitcoin's move to purpose-built SHA-256 ASICs around 2013 diverged from Ethereum's GPU path, meaning only the Ethereum side of crypto built the general-purpose GPU-cluster operator base that AI later needed.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: When evaluating a miner's AI-pivot credibility, its historical coin/hardware path predicts whether it holds transferable GPU-scale operating knowledge.
insights.locator: Bitcoin mining split early into two hardware paths

insights.claim: The same Nvidia CUDA-programmable parallel architecture that ran billions of identical mining hash calculations is efficient at matrix multiplication, the core operation underlying transformer training and inference, making crypto mining an accidental decade-long industrial stress test of AI's future hardware.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: CUDA's dual utility explains Nvidia's entrenched position across both eras and why software lock-in, not just silicon, underpins the moat.
insights.quote: Mining was an accidental decade-long industrial stress test of hardware AI would later require at massive scale.
insights.locator: The GeForce card's parallel processing architecture

themes.theme: Mining's Operational Inheritance and Head Start

insights.claim: Mining economics denominated in electricity forced operators into power procurement disciplines conventional data-center operators rarely develop: long-term power purchase agreements, direct substation access, and grid-operator relationships that take years to secure.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Pre-negotiated power contracts are the scarce, slow-to-replicate asset behind ex-miner valuations; watch power access, not land or capital, as the binding constraint.
insights.quote: An operation paying $0.04 per kilowatt-hour is profitable where one paying $0.08 is not
insights.locator: Mining economics are denominated in electricity

insights.claim: When OpenAI, Anthropic, Stability AI, and Inflection sought large GPU clusters in 2023 and found hyperscaler waitlists stretching months, former miners owned the only non-hyperscaler buildings already engineered for that thermal load.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Explains the demand pull that let ex-miners land frontier-lab contracts; scarcity of thermal-ready capacity is the deal-flow driver.
insights.locator: When a handful of well-funded AI startups

insights.claim: AI training requires thousands of GPUs coordinating gradient updates continuously over Nvidia InfiniBand at 400 gigabits per second or faster, a networking stack miners never built because rigs mine independently, but it took months of engineering rather than years, still yielding an 18-month head start.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Quantifies the durability of the miner advantage; the ~18-month lead is finite, so the moat is physical plant and power, not networking software.
insights.quote: The physical plant was ready. The networking and software stack required months of engineering work, not years.
insights.locator: Networking and orchestration is where the analogy

insights.claim: Crusoe pioneered a stranded-energy model by contracting to consume flared natural gas that oil producers were burning as waste, producing some of the lowest-cost compute in the market.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Stranded-energy sourcing is a differentiated cost lever; Crusoe's flared-gas access is a structural margin advantage worth tracking versus grid-dependent peers.
insights.quote: Crusoe went further, contracting to consume flared natural gas from oil fields that producers were literally burning as waste.
insights.locator: Mining economics are denominated in electricity

insights.claim: Google acquired DeepMind in 2014, employed more AI researchers than anyone, and invented the Transformer in 2017, yet withheld an aggressive consumer AI product because ChatGPT-style assistants threatened the search business that funded everything.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Classic innovator's dilemma; Google's structural conflict of interest is why incumbents ceded early AI ground and a lens for judging their catch-up credibility.
insights.quote: Nobody needs to “Google it” when they can ask Claude.
insights.locator: The hyperscalers were caught flat-footed

insights.claim: AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud spent fifteen years optimizing for CPU-dense web servers, databases, and VMs with predictable linear demand, so when ChatGPT sent GPU demand vertical they committed hundreds of billions to build data centers that cannot materialize on command.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Legacy CPU optimization is why hyperscalers lagged on GPU buildout, opening the window ex-miners exploited; buildout lead times gate their recovery.
insights.quote: Data centers don’t materialize on command regardless of how much money you throw at it.
insights.locator: Meanwhile AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud

insights.claim: Core Scientific filed for bankruptcy in December 2022 with $1.3 billion in debt as electricity bills exceeded mining revenue, and Compute North filed Chapter 11 in September 2022 after raising $385 million that year, failures of capital structure and leverage against a single volatile commodity rather than failures of infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: The distinction between capital-structure failure and infrastructure failure is the key diligence question for any leveraged compute play priced on a volatile input.
insights.quote: Not failures of infrastructure — failures of capital structure.
insights.locator: The casualties borrowed against cryptocurrency prices

insights.claim: Survivors Marathon Digital, Riot Platforms, and CleanSpark had balance sheets large enough to absorb the downturn, accumulated Bitcoin at depressed prices, rode the 2024 recovery, and now run hybrid models mining when Bitcoin economics favor it and pivoting to AI hosting when they do not.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Hybrid mine-or-host optionality is a real hedge; balance-sheet strength was the survival variable and remains the screen for cycle-resilient operators.
insights.locator: The survivors — Marathon Digital, Riot Platforms, CleanSpark

insights.claim: CoreWeave, founded as Atlantic Crypto in 2017, deliberately pivoted in 2019, three years before the crash, to exit mining and become a GPU cloud provider with an orchestration layer purpose-engineered for collective GPU computation rather than a mining byproduct.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: CoreWeave's early deliberate pivot, not accidental survival, distinguishes it from hybrid miners and underpins its premium positioning.
insights.quote: The orchestration layer it built wasn’t a mining byproduct; it was purpose-engineered for collective GPU computation
insights.locator: CoreWeave is the clearest example

insights.claim: CoreWeave joined the Nasdaq-100 on June 22, 2026, fifteen months after its IPO and nine years after its founders assembled their first GPU rig, with the ecosystem it anchors now worth well over $200 billion.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Marks the institutional legitimization of compute-real-estate; index inclusion pulls passive flows and reprices the whole ex-miner cohort.
insights.locator: Former Ethereum miner CoreWeave took its gold to Wall Street

insights.claim: In a single 48-hour window in April 2026 CoreWeave announced a multi-year Nvidia-GPU capacity deal with Anthropic for Claude inference and simultaneously closed a $21 billion expansion of its infrastructure partnership with Meta.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Establishes CoreWeave as the critical GPU supply link between Nvidia and top AI labs; concentration of marquee contracts signals pricing power but also customer concentration risk.
insights.locator: The distance traveled became clear

insights.claim: Crusoe sold its Bitcoin mining business in April 2024 to go all-in on AI, committing to a 1.2 gigawatt data center campus in Texas, while a restructured Core Scientific signed a $10.2 billion hosting contract with CoreWeave.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of the full mining-to-AI capital rotation; the $10.2B intra-cohort contract shows ex-miners are now each other's customers, amplifying sector interdependence.
insights.quote: Just as in the oil patch, the infrastructure outlasted the drillers who built it.
insights.locator: Crusoe sold its Bitcoin mining business

themes.theme: Nvidia's Wildcatter Alliance vs Custom Silicon

insights.claim: Hyperscalers are simultaneously Nvidia's largest customers and its most dangerous long-term competitors because Amazon has Trainium, Google has TPUs, and Microsoft is developing its own AI chips, so every dollar of compute on custom silicon is a dollar not spent on an H100 or B200.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Custom-silicon substitution is the core threat to Nvidia's TAM; track hyperscaler chip mix shift as the leading indicator of Nvidia demand erosion.
insights.quote: Every dollar of compute that runs on custom silicon is a dollar that doesn’t run on an H100 or B200.
insights.locator: That relationship runs deeper than preferred customer status

insights.claim: Nvidia strategically finances ex-miner 'wildcatters' because their all-Nvidia business models make them a loyal advanced column against hyperscaler custom silicon, exemplified by a January 2026 $2 billion private placement in CoreWeave plus a $6.3 billion take-or-pay capacity guarantee through 2032.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Nvidia's vendor financing props up CoreWeave demand and locks in a Nvidia-only channel; the take-or-pay backstop de-risks CoreWeave but signals circular financing worth scrutinizing.
insights.quote: The wildcatters drill the speculative wells. Nvidia, the equipment manufacturer, quietly financing the drilling.
insights.locator: In January 2026 it made a $2 billion private placement

insights.claim: Every megawatt of compute real estate represents a fixed allocation of overwhelmingly Nvidia GPU silicon, so the binding constraint on the industry is GPU allocation and power density, not land or capital.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Reframes data-center scarcity as a chip-allocation and power problem; preferred Nvidia allocation is the real moat to underwrite.
insights.quote: The constraint isn’t land or capital. It’s GPU allocation and power density.
insights.locator: Every megawatt of compute real estate capacity

insights.claim: Hyperscalers rent a virtualized software abstraction of a GPU rather than a physical one, adding latency that compounds across thousands of GPUs synchronizing millions of times per second, whereas CoreWeave inherited bare-metal operations from mining days because miners had no reason to virtualize a rig.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Bare-metal is a structural, hard-to-copy performance edge because hyperscalers built empires on virtualization they cannot easily remove; a durable differentiator for pure-play GPU clouds.
insights.quote: The hyperscalers built empires on virtualization and now can’t easily remove the foundation. The wildcatters never laid it in the first place.
insights.locator: The performance advantage that makes the wildcatters worth financing

insights.claim: By late 2025 miners and their AI-pivoted successors had announced $65 billion in contracts with major technology companies, and mining revenue for companies securing AI contracts is projected to fall from 85% of total revenue in early 2025 to under 20% by end of 2026.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Quantifies the revenue-mix inversion; a rapid shift to AI hosting reprices these companies off crypto beta and onto AI-infrastructure multiples.
insights.locator: By late 2025, miners and their AI-pivoted successors

insights.claim: AI hosting runs at 80 to 90 percent operating margin while Bitcoin mining runs near breakeven, making the migration a structural conclusion rather than a trend.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: The margin gulf is the economic engine forcing the pivot; hosting margins of 80-90% are the metric to verify against actual operator disclosures.
insights.quote: AI hosting runs at 80 to 90 percent operating margin. Bitcoin mining near breakeven. The migration isn’t a trend. It’s a structural conclusion.
insights.locator: By late 2025, miners and their AI-pivoted successors

themes.theme: The Bubble Question and Risk Rhyme

insights.claim: Nvidia lost $600 billion in market cap in a single session when DeepSeek demonstrated that capable inference could run on a fraction of the compute previously assumed necessary.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Efficiency breakthroughs are the tail risk to the whole compute-demand thesis; DeepSeek-style shocks can vaporize valuations premised on ever-rising compute intensity.
insights.locator: The AI market is asking a similar question

insights.claim: Hyperscalers committed a combined $300 billion-plus in AI capital expenditure in 2025 alone, a number priced for demand that has not fully materialized in revenue.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Flags a capex-versus-revenue gap that is the crux of the AI-infrastructure bubble debate; monitor whether AI revenue closes the gap on the assumed timeline.
insights.quote: a number priced for demand that hasn’t fully materialized in revenue
insights.locator: The hyperscalers have committed to a combined

insights.claim: CoreWeave still derived approximately two-thirds of its 2025 revenue from a single customer, Microsoft, whose own Azure infrastructure could not meet AI demand fast enough.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Severe customer concentration is CoreWeave's key idiosyncratic risk; the Microsoft dependency could reverse if Azure closes its GPU capacity gap.
insights.locator: CoreWeave still derived approximately two-thirds

insights.claim: The 2022 miners were not wrong about GPU infrastructure but about capital structure, and the parallel risk for 2025 AI infrastructure investors is being wrong about the timeline, competitive dynamics, or the assumption that Nvidia's pricing power is permanent.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Frames the specific ways the AI bet can fail without the thesis being wrong; Nvidia pricing-power permanence is the assumption most worth stress-testing.
insights.locator: But the crypto miners who went broke in 2022

insights.claim: The $200 billion compute real estate ecosystem was substantially financed by Bitcoin, an asset with no earnings, rent, or intrinsic utility whose value rests on institutionalized collective belief now embedded in a BlackRock ETF and sovereign wealth fund positions.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: The physical AI substrate's funding provenance ties its fate to crypto sentiment; a belief-driven capital base is a fragility investors should price.
insights.locator: The $200 billion compute real estate ecosystem

themes.theme: Compute Real Estate as an Asset Class

insights.claim: A new category called compute real estate has emerged at the intersection of semiconductor infrastructure, commercial real estate, and utility operations, with colocation giants structured as REITs, Equinix having converted in 2015 and Blackstone having acquired QTS for $10 billion.
insights.speaker: Jonah McLeod
insights.so_what: Compute real estate is becoming an institutional asset class; REIT structures and PE ownership create new vehicles for exposure to AI infrastructure buildout.
insights.quote: What emerged is a category that didn’t have a name five years ago: compute real estate.
insights.locator: What emerged is a category
